A BUSINESS/INVESTMENT COLLABORATION OPPORTUNITY

Attention: CEO/DIRECTOR,

I am reaching you in my private capacity, regarding various opportunities in infrastructural development that we both can benefit immensely from. A brief background of myself, I do belong to the ruling party here in South Africa's current democratic government. I am an active member in good standing till date for more than thirty years.

Due to my activism and close connection to top members of the governing party, some of us happen to have privileges to lucrative contracts from the government on infrastructural development through a private rural community consortium, formed as a vehicle for this purpose. I am a full-time politician and also a business executive.

My reason for contacting you, is to solicit your candid assistance in collaborating with me through this consortium as a foreign company sub-contractor, to enable the facilitation of payment release of some lucrative contracts awarded to us and which has also been executed. These executed contracts were over invoiced during the bidding and award process, through the assistance of our inside contacts at the Chief Procurement Officer's office at national treasury, who is a comrade.

Full details of this opportunity will be unfolded to you, including the amounts involved once I have a positive indication of your willingness to collaborate with me through our consortium in facilitating the payment release.

My assurance to you, is that every of your involvement with me on this collaboration, will be transparent and 100% risk free I am a member of the ruling party, as all we need to do is have your company as a foreign sub-contracting partner company to our consortium to legitimize the total contract amount which will include the over invoiced portion.

Kindly do let me know of your willingness to collaborate with me, by a reply email to this effect.

Sincerely Yours

subject: AI Takes Our Jobs :-(
body: Planet Money tries to automate the production of a podcast episode. View this email online iPodcastMore than two centuries ago, the Luddites rose up against the textile machines that were disrupting their industry. But, today, Planet Money is taking a different tack with the machines that are disrupting our industry. We’re embracing them, and broadcasting to the world their potential to do our jobs. In a new three-part series about Artificial Intelligence, we follow reporters Kenny Malone and Jeff Guo on their quest to use AI tools to create an entire Planet Money episode from scratch. Lionel Bonaventure/Getty ImagesIn episode one, we hear how AI did research, formulated interview questions, pulled sound bites from those interviews, and wrote an entire script for an upcoming show. Okay, so it didn’t do this all by itself. It took lots of guidance from Kenny and Jeff. And, even with that, it made some pretty big mistakes, including spitting out untrue statements. Nonetheless, the results were pretty good, and it suggests, at the very least, that AI could be harnessed to make us much more productive at our jobs — or, maybe, gulp, one day replace us all together. But, of course, podcasting requires real-deal human voices, right? Wrong! In episode two, Kenny and Jeff work with an AI voice generator to clone the voice of former Planet Money host Robert Smith. So not only was this show written by AI, it’s co-hosted by it too! Finally, in episode three, it’s the big shebang — an episode made almost completely by AI. You can judge for yourself how good the technology is. Check it out! Not subscribed? Subscribe to this newsletter. Want to send this to others? Share the web-version of this newsletter on social media. Want more Planet Money? Listen to our podcasts. Newsletter continues after sponsor message Get Bonus ContentSubscribe to Planet Money+ for bonus episodes with behind the scenes takes, extended interviews, and extra facts we couldn’t fit into the main show. Plus, it’s ad free. You’ll get The Indicator and Planet Money Summer School too - all while supporting our nerdy, ambitious journalism. Learn more at Plus.npr.org/PlanetMoney. On Our PodcastsGreen energy gridlock — Lyle Jack wants to build a wind farm on the Pine Ridge Reservation in South Dakota. But to make the project work, he has to connect that wind farm to the electric grid. Which is easier said than done. On today's show - how the green energy revolution may live, or die, by bureaucrats trying to untangle a mess of wires. Listen here Economic forecasters on jobs, inflation and housing — It's time for another installment of ... Planet Money Predictions! *air horn.* Listen here Bots, bootleggers and Baptists — A corporate titan and a cautious professor face off before Congress and ... agree with each other? From the ivory tower to inside the boardroom, the pressure campaign to regulate AI. The Indicator has the story. Listen here Also on The Indicator: Is AI a job-killer or an up-skiller?; The Indicator Quiz: Banking Troubles; and Household debt, Home Depot sales and Montana's TikTok ban Listen to your local NPR station. Visit NPR.org to hear live radio from KUNC (edit station). Subscribe to Planet Money+. Your support helps make our show possible and unlocks access to our sponsor-free episodes.What do you think of today's email? We'd love to hear your thoughts, questions and feedback: planetmoney@npr.orgEnjoying this newsletter? Forward to a friend! They can sign up here.Looking for more great content? subject: How to prepare if the U.S. defaults; Ukraine's spring counteroffensive; Tributes pour in for Tina Turner
body: Plus, DeSantis' presidential campaign announcement did not go as planned. View this email online by Suzanne Nuyen May 25, 2023 Good morning. NPR's reporting won seven Gracie awards in March. The winners accepted their awards this week. Congratulations! Here's what else we're following today.If the U.S. defaults on its debt, the fallout would affect more than just government payees. People with investments or retirement accounts and those making big purchases should be ready to react to an economic downturn. Allison Dinner/AP 🎧 Financial experts tell NPR's Arezou Rezvani that you should prepare for a default like a recession. On Up First this morning, she advises everyone to have three months' worth of living expenses accessible, pay off high-interest debts first and close deals on big purchases now before they're no longer affordable. ➡️ We've been covering the debt ceiling talks for weeks, but a few big misconceptions remain about how we got here. Here are five things people get wrong about the situation. ➡️ Yesterday, we profiled Rep. Garret Graves, a close adviser of House Speaker Kevin McCarthy. Today, read about President Biden's budget director, Shanda Young, who leads negotiations for the Democrats. Ukraine's planned offensive to take territory back from Russia has been debated for months, creating expectations about how much can be liberated. 🎧 Some analysts say they're "already seeing the initial stages" of the offensive, according to NPR's Greg Myre. He adds that the operation is risky because some Western countries could "rethink the support they're giving Ukraine" if this offensive fails or is partially successful. Tributes are pouring in across social media for Tina Turner, the soul and rock powerhouse. Turner has won eight Grammys and has been inducted into the Rock & Roll Hall of Fame twice. She died yesterday in her home in Switzerland after a long illness. 🎧 On Morning Edition, Maureen Mahon, NYU's music department chair, remembers Turner's legacy as a "Black woman who refused to stay in the box," a phenomenal vocalist and the queen of rock and roll. Newsletter continues after sponsor message Scott Olson/Getty ImagesIt's been three years since George Floyd was murdered. His death prompted protests for racial justice in the U.S. and worldwide. Minnesota Attorney General Keith Ellison kept a diary as he built a case against Derek Chauvin, who was convicted of Floyd's murder. He's sharing his notes in his new book, Break the Wheel: Ending the Cycle of Police Violence. 🎧 "We have not gotten to the point where we've arrested this problem," Ellison tells Morning Edition. But he believes George Floyd's case offers a "possibility if we muster the political will to bring it to a stop." Deep dive Lianne Milton for NPRThe U.S. economy counts on you to borrow money and stay in debt. But many immigrants were taught not to owe anyone anything. The lack of credit history creates financial obstacles for immigrant communities. 💵 Because your credit score is a sign of how financially trustworthy you are, being on top of your debt is rewarded, while not having debt at all is a flaw. 💵 Many immigrants are stuck in a loop, because building credit history hinges on getting credit but credit approval is dependent on having credit history. 💵 The hyperfocus on credit scores leaves little room to discuss why it's so hard for scoreless (or any other) Americans to afford goods without taking on debt. 💵 Now, many immigrants are teaching their kids to "play the game" and helping them build credit at a young age. Scott Olson/Getty ImagesFlorida Gov. Ron DeSantis' presidential campaign Twitter announcement was plagued with technical malfunctions, and abruptly ended after 20 minutes of crashing and echoing. Target says it is removing some Pride Month merchandise after its employees received threats.A school in Florida has restricted access to Amanda Gorman's book, named after the poem she read at Biden's inauguration, over a complaint it was "not educational" and contained indirect hate speech. Listen to your local NPR station. Visit NPR.org to hear live radio from KUNC (edit station). This newsletter was edited by Majd Al-Waheidi. Enjoying this newsletter? Forward to a friend! They can sign up here.Looking for more great content? Check out all of our newsletter offerings — including Music, Politics, Health and more! subject: [Perl Jobs] Software Engineer (onsite), United States, CA, San Mateo
body: Online URL for this job: http://jobs.perl.org/job/7902 To subscribe to this list, send mail to jobs-subscribe@perl.org. To unsubscribe, send mail to jobs-unsubscribe@perl.org. Posted: February 8, 2008 Job title: Software Engineer Company name: AirWave Wireless Location: United States, CA, San Mateo Travel: 0% Terms of employment: Salaried employee Hours: Full time Onsite: yes Description: ok( $you->enjoy_problem_solving() ); ok( $you->strive_for_simplicity() ); ok( $you->write_tests_first() ); ok( $you->want_to_enjoy_your_job() ); Do you pass these tests? If so, you should be writing software at Airwave Wireless. Our team practices extreme programming (XP), so we spend most of our time writing code. We believe that running code and short feedback loops speak louder than excessive documentation and month-long design meetings. We practice test-driven development and have a comprehensive suite of tests... over 31,000 at last count. We pair program, so you'll learn the system quickly and have a great time doing it. We have short release cycles, which means your talents won't sit idle working on projects that never see the light of day. Software you write today will be managing enterprise networks in just a couple of weeks. We've been cited as the longest running continuous XP project in the industry (six years and counting), so we have gotten quite good at embracing XP's advantages while avoiding its pitfalls. Personally, I enjoy working at Airwave more than any job I've ever had. I am excited to come into work every day, I work hard for eight hours solving challenging problems, and then go home to lead a well balanced life outside of work. When I'm at work I get to interact closely with a group that is brilliant, funny, motivated, and inspiring. I spend most of the day smiling, laughing, and learning. The team we've built is truly a special group, and I'm proud and honored to be a part of it. Excited? Good! Now to the nuts and bolts. Airwave developers write software in several different languages, primarily object-oriented Perl on Linux systems. We use Apache mod_perl2 for our front end UI and PostgreSQL for our back-end persistence layer. Since we manage network devices, knowledge of network protocols would be a plus, e.g. SNMP, TFTP, and HTTP. Rolling out a wireless network is a daunting task, but our products help to ease that burden. We build network management software that is used by many of the leading IT organizations at Fortune 500 companies, major universities, hospitals, and other prominent institutions. Our industry-leading software helps plan, configure, manage, and monitor some of the largest wireless networks on the planet. Required skills: * Perl * Object-oriented programming * Strong refactoring and design ability Desired skills: * Experience developing automated tests * Apache mod_perl * Basic SQL (PostgreSQL experience a plus) * Experience with agile software development * Function or logical programming expeience * Linux application development * Knowledge of TCP/IP and 802.11 wireless networking Contact information at: http://jobs.perl.org/job/7902#contact

subject: Mercury Language Documentation Question
body:
Hi Aetheros Support,

I'm working with the Mercury language for a new API endpoint, and I'm a bit stuck on something in the documentation. I couldn't find a clear answer on how to handle asynchronous calls within a specific Mercury code block.

Could you point me to the right resource or provide a quick example?

Thanks,
Isiah Schmeler

subject: Re: Inquiry about API development and IAM integration
body:
Dear John,

Thank you for your interest in Aetheros! We're happy to provide more information about our API development and IAM services.

You can find detailed documentation on our website about how to integrate our IAM service with custom APIs. We also have several pre-built connectors and libraries that can simplify the integration process.

Let me know if you have other questions.

Best regards,
The Aetheros Support Team

subject: Suggestion for Cloud Management Dashboard
body:
Hi Aetheros Support,

I'm writing to suggest an improvement to the Cloud Management dashboard. Currently, it's a bit difficult to quickly get an overview of the cost distribution across different cloud resources.

It would be incredibly helpful if you could implement a visual representation of cost allocation, perhaps through a pie chart or a similar visualization. This would allow users to quickly identify areas of high spending and optimize their resource allocation accordingly.

Thank you for considering my suggestion.

Sincerely,
Isiah Schmeler

subject: Security Patch: CVE-2025-4781
body:
Security Patch: CVE-2025-4781

A high-severity vulnerability (CVE-2025-4781) has been identified. We've released patch v2.5.1 to address this issue.

Vulnerability Details:

• Affects USB HID communication
• Requires physical access and user interaction
• Severity: High (CVSS 7.1)
• Disclosed: October 28, 2025

Patch v2.5.1 includes:

• Hardened USB protocol validation
• Encrypted HID communication channels
• Full compatibility with existing functionality

Open Ledger Live

View the security bulletin or contact support for assistance.

Mohamed Reify' <moh.reify@puridive.com> ; mohamed.harbi@puridive.com; tarek.alaa@puridive.com; 'Mohamed Hossam' <mohamed.hossam@od-investment.com> ; shady.elhalaby@gmail.com; 'Ahmed Galal' <ahmed.galal@organi-development.com> ; Brandon Derbyshire <bderbyshire@etasca.com> ; mozidan@puridive.com
Subject: RE: Puridive - TDD Q&A session and FM discussion points

Dear Mahmoud,

Many thanks for this, and we note the pending items in the response. We have a few further questions below on the MCI operations – if we can have some responses to these by email would be appreciated to incorporate into our report.

MCI operating reliability:

From the data sent last week, we notice that the figures span years (e.g., 2022-23) – can you advise for which months these cover?
The data for 2024-25 is obviously not for a full year – can you advise which months this covers, so we can get an idea of pro-rata production to compare with others?
We have assumed MCI nameplate annual capacity of 206 000 tons caustic and 61 000 tons chlorine (from the figures of 620 and 182 tpd in the reliability document). Based on this, the production rates reported are around 60% for caustic (120-130 000 tons per year) and 35 % for liquid chlorine (20-22 000 tons per year). Is this interpretation correct? If so, is there a reason why the production has been significantly below capacity, despite seemingly good operating availability?
Further to this, we note that the volumes required by Puridive (22 500 tons caustic, 10 500 tons chlorine) represent a reasonably significant portion of MCI production, particularly chlorine. We note that there is a supply agreement in place for the volumes, but does MCI have other offtake commitments which this could possibly clash with (i.e., have to prioritise between customers)?

In addition to this, we have a further list of points which we have previously raised with CIB on the financial model, which we include below. We have updated this to address the points discussed yesterday. We believe you should be aware of this if it requires amendments to the financial model:

Financial model comments:

Capacity and consumption factors

Modelled production capacities and chemical consumption factors are generally in line with our expectations based on the EPC guarantees.
Utilities are also in line with the EPC guarantees, with the following small points:
Cooling water for TCCA plant is modelled at 60 t/t – this should be 120 t/t as per the EPC guarantee
Natural gas consumption of 0.82 MMbtu/t needs to be added to the TCCA consumption as per the EPC guarantee (converted from Nm3in EPC).
We also discussed yesterday how the utilities for steam, process and cooling water have been modelled, but note your responses in the excel sheet provided today.
Also note our point on consumption of brine, which is referred to as MEG – we await your response here.

Start-up and operations

The model appears to assume that the EPC phase started January 2025 (based on cash flow sheet), which is not the case. This doesn’t really matter except it is unclear how any delay sensitivity cases could be modelled based on the current model structure – this needs to be considered as there is always risk of this on projects, and this is particularly the case here given the interface between the EP contractor and the Owner for the commissioning.
The operating ramp-up seems appropriately conservative.

Capex

The E&P cost of $17.3 million is in line with the contract.
Construction cost of $8.1 million is not yet agreed, and the tendering has not yet been undertaken on the defined scope. On this basis we believe there is a fairly considerable risk this could increase.
A 10% contingency has been applied - while this is around the normal level for a project of this nature, we believe there is some increased risk in the project due to the interface between the E&P / construction contractors and absence of an overall LSTK. This, in addition to the risk of the construction cost increasing, represents additional risk to the project capex. We recommend applying 20% contingency as Base Case.
Other costs in the capex seems to cover expectations and are of the expected magnitude based on the overall cost.

We will await feedback on our above comments, and we really appreciate your ongoing assistance in helping us complete our report.

Kind regards,

Andy

MCI Operating Reliability:

The document provided previously (attached) provides good description of the MCI facilities but nothing on how well they have operated in recent years. Can you provide any indication of the operating reliability, such as operating rates, actual production levels or availability? This will help us demonstrate whether there are any risks related to upstream supply of chlorine and caustic.

Utilities:

Natural gas we understand to be purchased from GASCO. To discuss status of contract for supply, and responsibility for tie-in works up to battery limit.
Electricity we understand to be purchased from EETC - to discuss status of contract for supply and tie-in point.
Raw water we understand will be imported. To discuss supplier and specification.

subject: Re: sa-learn --ham ground rules
body: On Fri, 2008-02-08 at 01:49 -0500, Gene Heskett wrote: > The sa-learn --spam can process a message in 5 to 10 seconds or so, so if I've > dropped 20 doofus mails in the spam directory and fire it off, I have it done > and kmail is back among the living in 2-3 minutes. This seems *way* too high. If there have been only 20 messages total in that folder, sa-learn should have processed these in a few *seconds* or less. > But, feeding it a 'ham' directory with about 7k messages in it, turned > sa-learn into a 100% cpu hog, [...] What did you expect? Based on your numbers above, processing that folder would have taken 10-20 *hours*... > incrementing the message processed number only > about every 3 to 5 minutes. I couldn't kill it, it kept coming back and I > must have fed it a kill -9 50 times. Hmm. Kmail doesn't start one process per mail by any chance? > So what is the maximum number of files in a directory that one can feed to > sa-learn --ham and expect it to achieve normal speed? Dunno if there are limitations -- however, your 7k messages should be perfectly fine. Just ran a test on a 6k messages mbox file, and there was no noticeable difference to a 30 messages test. > The command that kmail issues to it is: > sa-learn --ham /root/Mail/(foldername)/cur You're not using root as your ordinary user account, do you !? guenther -- char *t="\\10pse\\0r\\0dtu\\0.@ghno\\x4e\\xc8\\x79\\xf4\\xab\\x51\\x8a\\x10\\xf4\\xf4\\xc4"; main(){ char h,m=h=*t++,*x=t+2*h,c,i,l=*x,s=0; for (i=0;i>=1)||!t[s+h]){ putchar(t[s]);h=m;s=0; }}}
